title: AI Super-Sleuth Finds 100+ Hidden Planets — Some Are Really Weird!
summary: Imagine you have a giant photo album with millions of stars. Some of those stars might have planets, but spotting them is like finding a needle in a haystack. That's the problem astronomers faced with data from NASA's TESS satellite. TESS watches stars and looks for tiny dips in their light — a sign that a planet is crossing in front. But lots of things can fake that dip, like two stars dancing around each other. Enter RAVEN, a new AI brain built by scientists at the University of Warwick. They trained it with hundreds of thousands of fake planet signals so it could learn to tell real planets from imposters. And boy, did it deliver! RAVEN found 118 new planets and over 2,000 possible ones. Some are wild: ultra-short-period planets whip around their star in less than 24 hours. Others live in the 'Neptunian desert' — a zone where scientists thought planets couldn't survive. The team also measured how common these close-in planets are. About 1 in 10 Sun-like stars has one. That's way more precise than before. The best part? They shared their tools and catalogs so other scientists can hunt for more worlds. Who knows what else is hiding out there?
why_it_matters: Just like you search for hidden treasures, these new planets show us that our universe still has countless surprises waiting to be found — and one day, some of them might even be like Earth.

keywords.explanation: Scientists who study stars, planets, and outer space.
A NASA satellite that stares at stars to look for tiny dips in their light, which can mean a planet is passing by.
Small drops in a star's brightness that may be caused by a planet crossing in front of it.
A smart AI computer program trained to tell real planet signals from fake ones.
Planets that race around their star in less than one Earth day.
A dangerous region close to a star where scientists thought planets could not survive.

What did the scientists share with others?

background_read: TESS (the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) is a NASA space telescope launched in 2018 to find planets around other stars. It watches large parts of the sky and records tiny dips of starlight.
For a long time, finding new planets was slow work because scientists had to check each signal by hand. Now, computers trained on fake and real signals can do the job much faster.
The 'Neptunian desert' isn't a real desert; it's a name for an area very close to a star where planets like Neptune seem to be rare. Finding planets in this zone surprises astronomers.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists at the University of Warwick built the AI brain called RAVEN.
WHAT: RAVEN discovered 118 new planets and over 2,000 possible ones, including some very strange worlds.
WHERE: Using data from NASA's TESS satellite that watches stars across the whole sky.
WHY: To separate real planet signals from fakes and to measure how common small, close-in planets are.

perspectives.description: Finding so many new planets at once is thrilling. It proves that space still holds unknown worlds that could teach us about how planets form.
Even a smart AI can be fooled. Scientists will double‑check each candidate to make sure no real planet is missed and no fake one is counted.
By giving their tools and lists to others, the team hopes that many more eyes will join the hunt, making discoveries faster and more exciting.
